key_insight: In neutron-irradiated tungsten, strain rate and indentation length scale affect nanoindentation response in different ways: strain rate mainly changes flow stress, while depth controls plastic-zone growth, pile-up, and GND buildup.
engineering_relevance: A calibrated strain-gradient crystal plasticity model can predict irradiation-hardened tungsten behavior across loading rates and length scales, supporting more reliable constitutive models for transient service conditions.
method: Spherical nanoindentation on unirradiated and neutron-irradiated single-crystal tungsten over 3.2e-5 to 3.2e-3 s^-1
AFM and HR-EBSD measurements of pile-up, residual lattice strain, and GND distributions
Strain-gradient CPFE with thermally activated slip, GND hardening, irradiation obstacle hardening, and strain-dependent softening
evidence_signal: The model was calibrated using one experimental condition and then validated across all remaining strain rates without further parameter adjustment, while also reproducing polycrystal cube compression behavior.
watch_item: The framework is calibrated on nanoindentation data and may require rechecking before use on different irradiation states, temperatures, or loading histories.
paper_title: Decoupling Strain-Rate Sensitivity and Deformation Length Scale Effects in Neutron-Irradiated Tungsten: A Coupled Nano-Indentation, HR-EBSD and Crystal Plasticity Study
